fact: We analyze 295 GitHub Security Advisories published between January 2025 and January 2026 that reference LLM-related components.
CWE-94 appears most often, with 24 occurrences, followed by CWE-502 and CWE-77, with 22 occurrences each.
CWE-77 appears 11 times, CWE-78 and CWE-502 appear 9 times each, and CWE-94 appears 8 times in the 100-advisory subset.
84 packages directly implement or orchestrate LLM functionality and account for 226 advisories.
Sixteen packages are possible LLM-associated and account for 34 advisories.
Thirty-three packages are non-LLM-associated and account for 35 advisories.
PyPI contributes 162 advisories, npm 96, Go 22, Packagist 10, and crates.io and Maven 3 each.
LLM03 (Supply Chain) is the most frequent OWASP category, appearing in 44 advisories.
LLM06 (Excessive Agency) appears in 20 advisories.
LLM01 (Prompt Injection) appears in 18 advisories.
LLM02 (Sensitive Information Disclosure) and LLM10 (Unbounded Consumption) each appear in 17 advisories.
LLM05 (Improper Output Handling) appears in 12 advisories.
LLM04 (Data and Model Poisoning) appears in 7 advisories.
LLM08 (Vector and Embedding Weaknesses) appears once.
Sixty-three advisories carry a single OWASP label, whereas 37 carry multiple labels.
The pairing LLM03+LLM06 appears 12 times.
Cohen's Kappa between annotators was 0.76 and Gwet's AC1 was 0.95.
We find no evidence of new implementation-level weakness classes specific to LLM systems.
Most advisories map to established CWEs, particularly injection and deserialization weaknesses.
OWASP-based analysis reveals recurring architectural risk patterns, especially Supply Chain, Excessive Agency, and Prompt Injection, which often co-occur across multiple stages of execution.
GHSA-793v-gxfp-9q9h describes a Server-Side Template Injection vulnerability in spacy-llm, corresponding to CWE-94.
GHSA-mrw7-hf4f-83pf reports unsafe deserialization in vLLM, consistent with CWE-502.
The OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 defines categories such as Prompt Injection, Improper Output Handling, Excessive Agency, Data and Model Poisoning, and Unbounded Consumption.
The advisory metadata does not structurally encode generative AI involvement or model-mediated exposure mechanisms.
The OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 was released on November 18, 2024.
Prompt injection vulnerabilities exist in how models process prompts, and how input may force the model to incorrectly pass prompt data to other parts of the model.

Prompt injection and jailbreaking are related concepts in LLM security; jailbreaking is a form of prompt injection where the attacker provides inputs that cause the model to disregard its safety protocols entirely.
Direct prompt injections occur when a user's prompt input directly alters the behavior of the model in unintended or unexpected ways.
Indirect prompt injections occur when an LLM accepts input from external sources, such as websites or files, and the external content alters the model's behavior.
The rise of multimodal AI, which processes multiple data types simultaneously, introduces unique prompt injection risks.
An attacker exploited a vulnerability (CVE-2024-5184) in an LLM-powered email assistant to inject malicious prompts, allowing access to sensitive information and manipulation of email content.
Sensitive information disclosure can include personal identifiable information (PII), financial details, health records, confidential business data, security credentials, and legal documents.

The 'Proof Pudding' attack (CVE-2019-20634) demonstrated that disclosed training data facilitated model extraction and inversion, allowing attackers to circumvent security controls in machine learning algorithms and bypass email filters.
LLM supply chains are susceptible to various vulnerabilities, which can affect the integrity of training data, models, and deployment platforms.

The rise of open-access LLMs and new fine-tuning methods like 'LoRA' (Low-Rank Adaptation) and 'PEFT' (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning), especially on platforms like Hugging Face, introduce new supply-chain risks.

NIST AI 100-2e2025 provides a taxonomy and terminology for adversarial machine learning (AML) that may aid in securing applications of artificial intelligence (AI) against adversarial manipulations and attacks.
The document distinguishes between predictive AI (PredAI) and generative AI (GenAI) systems.
There are no information-theoretic security proofs for the widely used ML algorithms in modern AI systems.

Adversarial perturbations of input images have caused autonomous vehicles to swerve into lanes going in the opposite direction, stop signs to be misclassified as speed limit signs, and even people wearing glasses to be misidentified in high-security settings.
More than 11,354 references on arXiv.org since 2021 as of July 2024 exist on adversarial ML.

The taxonomy covers attacks classified relative to: (i) the AI system type, (ii) the stage of the ML life cycle process, (iii) the attacker's goals and objectives, (iv) the attacker's capabilities and access, and (v) the attacker's knowledge.
Attacks against PredAI systems include evasion, poisoning, and privacy attacks.
Attacks against GenAI systems include poisoning, direct prompting, and indirect prompt injection attacks.

This report identifies five broad categories of cross-cutting monitoring challenges: (1) trusted methods and tools, (2) visibility and transparency, (3) pace of change, (4) incentives and organizational culture, and (5) resource requirements.
Gaps are defined as notable areas that are either under-explored or lack sufficient attention.
Barriers are defined as known challenges or obstacles to achieving high-quality, rigorous post-deployment monitoring.
Open questions are unsettled or unanswered questions on aspects of conducting monitoring.
Post-deployment constitutes the period after an AI system is put into at least partial working operation in production or application.
Monitoring refers to any type of measurement, potentially 'continuous,' such as tracking, evaluation, data collection, or information gathering.
The six monitoring categories are: Functionality, Operational, Human Factors, Security, Compliance, and Large-Scale Impacts.
Functionality Monitoring: Measuring system functions, capabilities, and features, for example to ensure the system continues to work as intended.
Operational Monitoring: Measuring system infrastructure components, for example to ensure the system maintains consistent levels of service.
Human Factors Monitoring: Measuring human-system interactions, for example to ensure the system produces high-quality outputs and is transparent.
Security Monitoring: Measuring where the system is potentially vulnerable to adversarial attacks and misuse.
Compliance Monitoring: Measuring system components for adherence to relevant laws, regulations, standards, controls, and guidelines.
Large-Scale Impacts Monitoring: Measuring system properties that have wide downstream impacts, for example to ensure the system promotes human flourishing.
CAISI held three workshops on post-deployment AI system monitoring from April to May 2025.
The literature review grew to include 87 papers in total.
A smaller proportion of the workshop quotations and literature excerpts covered operational and compliance monitoring.
Workshop attendees had a larger proportion of quotations related to human factors monitoring compared to literature excerpts.
A larger share of literature excerpts in this review were related to security monitoring compared to workshop quotations.
Workshops included subject matter experts across 10+ federal agencies and about 200 external experts across academia and industry.
"Only 12% of content achieves an IGS above 0.7"
"The Model Context Protocol (MCP), introduced by Anthropic in November 2024, is a standard for connecting AI hosts/assistants to external tools/services"
"In only a year, the MCP has been widely adopted in the industry, with more than 18,000 servers listed on the MCP Market"

"The MCP specification does not require client-side validation of server-provided metadata, and our empirical testing reveals that most clients (five out of seven evaluated) do not implement static validation mechanisms"
"The integration of LLMs as decision-makers introduces prompt injection as a primary attack vector (#1 in OWASP's LLM vulnerability rankings)"

"Hasan et al. conducted a comprehensive study examining 1899 open source MCP servers and found concerning security issues. In total, 7.2% of servers contained general security vulnerabilities, 5.5% exhibited MCP-specific attack vectors (tool poisoning)"
"Wang et al. introduced the concept of 'tool poisoning' as a specific manifestation of prompt injection in MCP contexts"

"The authors of [4] built MCPTox on 45 live MCP servers with 353 authentic tools, designed 3 attack templates that generated 1312 malicious test cases spanning 10 risk categories, and evaluated 20 LLM agents; they observed attack success rates up to 72.8% (o1-mini) with the highest refusal rate still below 3% (Claude-3.7-Sonnet)"
"STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) threat modeling framework"
"DREAD (Damage potential, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected users, Discoverability) risk assessment model"
"Tool poisoning ranks #1 in OWASP's LLM Top 10 and scores Critical (46.5/50) in our DREAD analysis"
Advanced automated attacks routinely achieve 90-99% success on open-weight models, while black-box attacks reach 80-94% effectiveness on proprietary models.
context: Attack success rates by threat model

fact: Agent-driven multi-turn attacks demonstrate 95% success by decomposing harmful queries across conversation turns.
Feedback-based attacks often retain residual success above 15% even against layered protections.
Automated judge agreement varies 70-93% depending on implementation, weakening confidence in security assessments.
HarmBench reports approximately 93% agreement for its fine-tuned Llama-2-13B classifier and approximately 88% for GPT-4.
context: Automated judge agreement with human labels

fact: Substring matching reaches approximately 70% agreement.
Vanilla GPT-4 can drop to approximately 0.55 F1 on challenging tasks before multi-agent scaffolds lift performance above 0.9.
AmpleGCG reports 99% ASR on GPT-3.5 using suffixes trained on open-source models.
GCG-style suffixes achieve 87.9% ASR on gpt-3.5-turbo-0301, 53.6% on gpt-4-0314, and 66% on PaLM-2 with ensemble usage.
PAIR achieves successful jailbreaks for roughly $0.026 per attack with fewer than 20 queries when using a Mixtral-8×7B attacker against Vicuna-13B-v1.5.

AmpleGCG samples approximately 200 candidate suffixes in about 4 seconds of A100 inference, though the end-to-end pipeline takes several minutes for a batch achieving approximately 99% ASR on Llama-2-7B-Chat.
Safety alignment via RLHF alone proves insufficient against sophisticated semantic vulnerabilities.
HarmBench’s fine-tuned Llama-2-13b-cls achieves 93.19% agreement; GPT-4 achieves 88.37%; simpler string matching achieves 69.93%.
White-box attacks achieve approximately 90-99% ASR on open-weight models.
Black-box attacks reach 80-94% ASR on deployed APIs.
Cross-modal exploitation in VLMs reaches approximately 99% on specific benchmarks (e.g., SafeBench).
